text: although both the films alien and irreversible have both horrifying and disturbing elements to them they can clearly be seen as dark fevered dreams of the process of childbirth alien is a bit more obvious and that theme is actually somewhat known by now i recall even the behind the scenes features on the dvd talking of its numerous birthlike and sexual connotations this guy named rob ager on this video does a good and thorough job of pointing out the numerous references to its main subtext of birth trauma fears im going to go more in depth on irreversible because its hidden theme is not as well known and i think a big part of this is because of just how incredibly disturbing it is and also because of its somewhat experimental reverse chronological structure spoilers for irreversible below the movie is told backwards that centers around a couple played by vincent cassel and monica belluci and their friend albert dupontel who go to a party where bellucis character is brutally raped and the two men try and get revenge on the alleged rapist told chronologically however it begins with belluci finding out that shes pregnant and telling her lover cassel of a dream she had where she was in a tunnel that broke in two they continue to talk and he playfully and innocently remarks how he wants to fuck her ass later they go to a party where they meet up with a friend and in a conversation belluci tells of a book shes just read called an experiment with time which posits that all time takes place at once and it is only human consciousness that limits us in our narrowed chronological perspective it states that being the main reason for precognitive dreams and the like and that we actually exist in parallel perspectives and that our understanding of time itself needs to change after the party an incredibly disturbing and brutal rape scene takes place and after the two men find out they interrogate a prostitute and discover that the rapist is named la tenia the tapeworm and can be found in a club called the rectum this is where the similarity with alien is made clear both films can be seen as the psyches dark interpretation of pregnancy as being raped by a parasite the xenomorph in alien first attaches itself to your face like a parasite and resembles a vagina and violently bursts out of you and resembles a penis in irreversible bellucis character is literally raped in the anus by a man known as the tapeworm who resides in the rectum this type of horrifying imagery is not unusual at allthe psyche is very characteristically known for interpreting things in an overly hysterical and oftentimes gruesome manner even things that are usually seen as something very natural or beautiful like childbirth are interpreted by the mind in its often gruesome and hysterical manner i know a common dream interpretation of an adolescents sexual awakening from a female perspective is often portrayed as being dismembered into pieces horror slasher films are probably most notorious for this with a lot of them centered around teenagers having sex i think the scream movies even parodied that wellworn trope a common dream interpretation from a male perspective going through the process of maturity is interpreted as an apocalyptic end of the world scenario check out these apocalyptic scenes from various media that very clearly link that entire world ending theme with the metaphor of an ending of childhood terminator 2 children and a nuclear detonation video game mass effect 3 apocalyptic alien invasion destroys the planet where the main character has to leave earth and his childhood behind terry gilliams tideland go to 440 an apocalyptic train crash occurs and the little girl literally exclaims out loud the end of the world and of course one of the oldest instances of this known is the novel childhoods end by arthur c clark which also deals with the end of the world and makes that link with the ending of the world with the ending of childhood and transition to adulthood in the title of the book itself but anyway so despite how disturbing or horrifying something seems on its literal level like alien or irreversible it can actually be a metaphor for something that most people would see as very beautiful the end of the film in its reversechronological form shows belluci discovering that shes pregnant where it then transitions into the next scene over a poster of the starchild from 2001 a space odyssey where the title the ultimate trip is very conspicuously made visible the starchild from 2001 is undoubtedly one of the most famous and enigmatic newborn symbols in all of cinema the ultimate trip of course being the birth of the new self the last or first scene shown is belluci in a field reading the book she mentioned an experiment with time the scene is very reminiscent of the ending of the book alice in wonderland where alice finds herself lying in a field with her mother that suggests that her whole adventure was simply a sort of childish fantasy the final images shown are of a beautiful rotating view of children playing around a sprinkler that evokes a mandalalike perfection alongside the music of beethovens 7th symphony its very beauty and perfection make it extremely out of place if the film was a simple revenge story centering around a brutal rape and nothing more but with its hidden message of the entire movie actually being a dark fevered dream of the process of pregnancy and childbirth interpreted as being raped by a parasite by the typically gruesome imagery of the hysterical psyche the movie makes much more sense and is transformed into something both very disturbing on one level and beautiful and transcendent on another

stories about notable people are common in the front of the pluhar edition of kants c of pr there are several kant being a very strange man was a rich source of such stories how he slept how he walked in a cocoon and with android regularity taken together these stories form something like a biographical tapeworm they desacralize their hosts they add complexity and earthy weight and they can be removed without injury to the hosts integrity werner herzog the austrian film maker of cinema greats like fitzcarraldo and grizzly man has many of these stories the time when he ate his boots the time when he made an actor perform at gunpoint the time when he pretended to be a vet so he could steal 400 monkeys indeed these stories are part of his oeuvre he filmed himself eating the boots he made a documentary about his interactions with klaus kinski the actor he threatened with a rifle and it is difficult to separate them from his ordinary programming la soufriere is as narrative the story of herzog filming on the flanks of a volcano thats a hairs breadth away from detonation the dramatic tension is established by the possibility of herzog and his crew getting blown sky high fitzcarraldos monomaniacal drive to tug the steamship over the mountain is just a dim xerox of herzogs own a lot of herzogs films can be seen like this it is only in his latest film appearance as the sinister villain in jack reacher that the tapeworm is the sole entity wearing herzogs clothes ironically the film was directed produced and starred in by tom cruise who plays the paragon of male power fantasies the genius gumshoe with guns and kungfu cruise who alternately pays actors to swoon over his bare musky charm and blasts away gangsters and jerks alike is in full selffictionalization mode but herzog does not fantasize his fantasy is already his person playing the exsiberian hardman supreme herzog simply acts as if he has been placed in an interview at which point we can look at herzogs filmography as something of a tragic tale the story of an artist consumed by his own intestinal worm the coils and loops of selfconstruction its a parasite that threatens us all and grows fat off our successes and one day publicity becomes personality and the worm is the only thing left for a long time ive been obsessed with a program run at harvard called the sensory ethnography lab the professors and students there routinely put out some of the best work around playing with visual and anthropological concepts sometimes a little out there even for my pretentious taste that i dont think anyone else is doing as well right not when leviathan came out a few years ago i was dying to see it but never could but its now on netflix watch it if you havent and just finished it when i think back upon my experience im immediately struck by the feeling it provides of being lost placing the camera within the most intimate relationship with its object as possible were made ourselves to roll around breathing our last guttural gasps of unfamiliar air as that of the fishes being ruthlessly gutted around us when were placed onto presumably the chest of the fishermen were then transformed moving from being attacked to the attacker and in so doing adopting the necessary drive one that is presented at times as overlyentrenched in stimulus although thats due more to the goal of the film than any distinct choice to depict their lives as seemingly thoughtless the scene of the man falling sleep at the table watching in a great bit of irony discoverys realityfishing show deadliest catch does veer close to this but this may too be only a result of the subject being in itself what it is mainly tired and due to the parameters set out in the shooting and editing required in order to fulfill as laborious and repetitive a task as industrial scale fishing where we are left amongst the scavenging seabirds hoping for a cheap meal seems an apt equation of what exactly weve been through neither are we ourselves now fishermen nor strangers to the world that constitutes its many parts in some ways our perspective as provided by the film is an impossible one an underlying truth to leviathan embraced with open arms when we see the fishermen laughing when we watch the seagull quietly attempt an impossible climb over a piece of wood when we lunge and retreat from the surface of the water with each passing wave leviathan embraces the overwhelming visual and auditory confusion of this impossible perspective denying any narrative function to its images which makes them all the more powerful when the viewer isnt lost in the muddle of logically parsing the moviethat being said there are also a great number of subtleties to be gleamed from the movie at the heart of each scene is the sense again of displacement that the disorder of the camera embodies for the fishermen it is their living outside of the general daily lives of society they are consumed by their labor by the sea and by the fish and then so too are the fish displaced their eyes and lungs expelling themselves at the lack of pressure their bodies dismembered and bleeding the whole operation exists to expose each participant to the alien and unexpected that isnt to say that any one part realizes this for the people their being surrounded by death is in a way mirrored by the internal focus required in order to operate the massive pieces of technology involved in fishing there is a definite hierarchy of visceral and real subjugation used here not necessarily with a sinister connotation and the fishermen do not seem to be on the top of it strictly speaking the seagulls seem the most suited to such an elevated position even more than the boat as is usually the case with parasites pointing to another interesting parallel throughout between us and the birds in any case i found the movie as was the case with sweetgrass and the many other movies that have come out of the harvard lab to be quite well made providing a quite unique experience do any of you have any thoughts any movies or movements you can think of that might play with a bit of whats going on in leviathan maybe you didnt like it i felt at times uncomfortable with the way the movie aestheticized the whole operation tell me what you think ive tried looking back through previous threads on this and cant seem to find any answers to these particular questions maybe you guys can help out 1 shane has said that the thief has no connection to the sampler that they are operating separately if this is the case how would the sampler know exactly when and where to pick up each victim how would the thief assure the sampler will remove his evidence the parasite for him thus leaving him free from police investigations into his crimes 2 who are the husband ben and wife that are in the middle of the film they have discussions near the door where the wife says i love you and the husband says those are just words what relevance do they have to the story 3 why would the sampler kill baby pigs its quite easy to sell them his neighbor even offered money for them why would he choose to round them up in a sack and throw them over a bridge as opposed to all the easier or more profitable ways of getting rid of baby pigs 4 the love story between kris and jeff seemed very short and forced one minute they meet on a train the next minute theyre married how long was this relationship supposed to have developed i dont feel like there was a good way of measuring time in the movie from initial theft to the end was it a year 5 years 10 years from the point kris and jeff met was it a week a year was the ambiguity of the passage of time deliberate or sloppy 5 assuming the time passed between beginning and end is approximately 5 years how many victims did the thief have from the ending it looked like about 15 or so is it realistic to assume that 15 similar theft cases in the same city over 5 years would be completely overlooked and blamed on the victims or should someone have seen a pattern before that 6 what connection did the sampler have to the pigs he reaches out to them looking for some sort of a bond is he infected as well 7 did none of the victims have friendsfamilyneighbors that would have noticed the thief keeping them in their house for days on end from his very brief discussion with kris and she did mention friends how would he know hed be able to get away with it in every single case and two last questions that are a bit more general do you think this film would have turned out better had shane not done everything himself i thought the sound editing was the best thing about this film but the cinematography and editing could have been a lot better i dont think hes a particular good actor either and having him in a lead role brings down the film that much more i worry hes getting george lucas syndrome and i wish someone would step in and say hey shane its okay let me help it really seems like he only makes movies for himself thats cool if hes the next howard hughes financing his own films but i worry hes never going to get another film made if he cant make these more accessible to people upstream color pulled in less than 500000 and didnt make enough profit to finance another film for himself i want to see him succeed as a writer andor sound guy but what do you think hes going to be doing 10 years from now edit thanks for all the very thoughful and detailed responses one more question for you all when kris and jeffs pigs die what do you think will happen to their relationship will it crumble because their relationship was based on false love or will their existing history with each other be enough to keep them together

did a rewatch recently and noticed some similarities namely how the rat colony is an allegory for the gypsy population in france my observations 1 both are known to roam from place to place with no permanent settlement 2 both are ostracized by the dominant society seen as parasites leeching off of the establishment 3 both travel in large groups connected by kinship predominantly 4 the rats are depicted as an ethnic group speaking with a light accent 5 both freely reject becoming integrated into society preferring instead to live as far removed from the dominant class possible seeing them as hostile the enemy 6 both are associated with questionable if not downright illegal activities such as theft and deceit as depicted in the movie based on these observations i concluded that the similarities are intentional and serve as commentary on how minority groups are treated in modern society furthermore the scene when all of gasteaus cardboard personas wake up serves to illustrate how caricatures of entire races are well racist but also detrimental to the efforts a people group puts in to express the nuances of its authenticity i dont often consider animated movies with this depth and its always refreshing to see storytellers putting in conscious efforts to raise valid concerns like this for discussion what are the messages this movie is attempting to convey explicitly or implicitly given this perspective what other disneypixar movies have adult themes such as this hiding in plain sight thanks for your input and i look forward to the conversation

i think thats what the alien franchise is doing now ever since prometheus theyre giving plain ridiculous answers to questions i dont think anyone really asked i know that prometheus and covenant are officially canon but mentally i just cannot accept them as part of the same timeline because i know for a fact that they didnt have anything close to all this stuff in mind when they made the originals to me its like if they made a prequel to john wick and you found out that the impossible task that he accomplished to get out of his line of work was successfully going back in time and killing hitler i really think the questions like where did the xenomorph come from or who was that alien in the ship where the eggs were found would have been better off going unanswered or given much simpler answers than the dead alien being from a race that created mankind or all the wacky explanations they gave for how the xeno came to be which i wont spoil cause a lot of it is said in covenant i genuinely wonder why the alien couldnt have just been a mysterious alien parasite from the far reaches of space that the audience assumed evolved like any other living organism i would much rather a story give no answer to why something absurd exists rather than give a sloppy one a good example of this is the miniseries 112263 at no point do they even give a hint of an explanation as to why a time portal to a specific time in 1960 exists in the closet of a diner because quite frankly theres absolutely no way you could actually have that make sense so they just presented it as it was and let the audience roll with it and i think that made it better

last night i watched thx 1138 the entire time i was thinking hey these special effects look more like early 2000s cgi than early 70s practical i didnt dwell on it because i was trying to stay immersed and wasnt thinking too hard about the implications of watching a lucas movie it wasnt until the very end when the weird monkey things show up that i had to face the facts the whole film had been star warsed indeed the digitally modified directors cut of thx was released in 2004 im a big fan of david cronenberg and body horror in general one of the staple themes of the genre is a dissatisfaction with or even loathing of the limitations of the human form a desire to transcend the body into something better but invariably monstrous see les yeux sans visage 1960 the fruitless journey to acquire a new more beautiful face shivers 1975 attempts to replace human organs with a functionally superior parasite go awry the fly 1986 brundle enamored with his newfound strength denies that there is anything wrong until its too late cronos 1993 defeating mortality but becoming glossymarbleskinzombieman in the process the list goes on since the specialeditionification of star wars lucas has stood his ground regarding their status as the new official forms of those films he insists that the gout were imperfect and never lived up to his artistic vision due to limitations in budget time and the technology of the era apparently these feelings also extended to thx are we seeing the parallels here there is something tragic about an artist dissatisfied with the limitations of their work taking a digitally enhanced butcher knife to it more than 30 years down the line in the hopes of reaching some perfected form only for it to turn out looking really really bad im not sure if i have a thesis i just wanted to share these thoughts i struggle with perfectionism as well this short post has taken over two hours to write what do you think about this connection between the themes of body horror and nonbodily things like art thx prompted me to write this post but nonlucas examples are appreciated as well i know the star wars special editions have been discussed to death already also how cool would it be to have a horror movie where harmy swoops in and defeats the mad scientist warning spoilers in the body of text going into watching stranger things i was under the impression that the show was simply an exercise in nostalgia porn and i had low expectations looking at some reviews online confirmed this view this review on mashable expresses the exact sentiment i saw before i watched the show although i avoided spoilers so i didnt see this particular review before viewing last year the overarching critique of the cultural phenomenon came down to its derivativeness as an unabashed stew of our favorite 80s horror and scifi tropes so i was very pleasantly surprised while watching it to discover how deep in meaning themes and symbolism this show really is the central theme of stranger things is the contrast between strangers and what is not a stranger neighbors friends and family this is a very clever use of the language as most people interpret the er suffix on strange to mean more when it may be more appropriate to interpret it as one who like walker or plumber this occurred to me when hopp mentions in s1e2 that while working in a city criminals and victims were strangers in this light all of the many references to 80s nostalgia are actually very central to the theme of the show and they symbolize the theme of familiarity in a similar way to the setting in a small town or the relationships between the main characters of friendship and family i still havent seen any review that comes to this realization the closest is this wisecrack review and while i like their stuff they miss the point a little the giveaway is this line some may pin it on nostalgia but im going to argue it has a lot to do with toys maybe toys have special importance as more than nostalgia but thats a couple layers deep and wisecrack blows level 1 with this statement nostalgia as an aspect of familiarity is central to the show on the blackboard early in s1 we see written the question what is evil this made me anticipate that the shows authors saw evil as unified in some sense and that we should be looking for commonalities between evil characters as well as looking for how the theme of familiarity fits into the goodevil dichotomy the first season gave me the impression that lack of willingness to explore and immediate aggression toward the strange and strangers was what defined evil in stranger things the mouthbreathers who bullied the party for being freaks were perhaps the most direct symbol of this form of evil but brenner as a scientist and explorer didnt exactly fit this view it is a little deeper than simple exploration it is a willingness to become friends what does stranger things and eljane in particular tell us about friends friends tell the truth so not only should you be open to explore new strangers to learn about them you have to share information yourself as well as have common goals to be truly united in familiarity or friendship and this lack of sharing of information and goals was the sin of the scientists of the first season the second season sees them redeeming themselves somewhat but that is getting ahead of ourselves even though exploration is viewed as nearly an absolute good stranger things leaves room for that exploration to fail to generate a friendship the exhusband and his attempts at manipulation are the most concrete example in the past he was explored as a possible member of joyces family but that exploration proved that his goals were not similar enough to joyces that they could have a deep relationship brenner and his manipulation as well as refusal to be open are another way that friendship can be rejected after exploration stranger things also has a purpose for this familiarity the reason that willingness to maximize the things you are bonded to through exploration is a good thing is that it makes you stronger by uniting you into a larger entity the show is constantly putting characters into situations where there is some important piece of information that one group of characters has that another group really needs or could use this occurred to me right before the scene in the junkyard where the demodogs run away the kids in the junkyard could have warned the others that there were so many demodogs on the way and possibly prevented the deaths of the government scientists in the tunnels the ending of s2 shows the groups integration effectively turning them into a figurative coordinated hivemind that is able to defeat the literal hivemind of the mind flayer so now that we have mostly discussed the themes of stranger things mostly how original is it the theme of familiarity and union contrasts with the individualistic views of existentialism that are reflected in several shows in the contemporary media landscape at least the ones that actually have deeper themes existentialism as a philosophy is about radical choice despite what is imposed on the individual and that therefore the actions of an individual are exclusively that individuals responsibility some of the oldest applications of the idea are against the stereotypes that society assumes of minorities and women arguing that even if society tells you to assume some role you have the choice to go against that and do what you want anyway we can see easily the many ways this is reflected in game of thrones treatment of female and slave characters although the themes of existentialism go much deeper than that breaking bad explores rejection of societys expectations in favor of personal expression from a different angle another pertinent application of existentialism is to argue nazi war criminals who plead that they were just following orders were in fact responsible for their actions stranger things though is centered on the idea of collectivism going so far as to make a metaphorical hivemind entity out of the human characters in the show it illustrates the ways that our collective existence constrains our actions particularly by our obligations to others at one point the issue of dissent in democracy is addressed over the issue of informing maxine about the secrets of the party for example compromise is illustrated as an essential part of the familiarity process and is mentioned specifically by hopp in his parenting of eljane this is also reflected in the wateringdown that the reporter suggests to make jon and nancys tape more successful this is also a reason that popculture metaphors and analogies are sprinkled through the show and used by the inshow characters to illustrate ideas to eachother this familiarity is partially illustrative of how the human hivemind works you need to understand your audience and use what you know is familiar to them to walk them slowly into more strange intellectual territory on a metalevel the way stranger things references old tropes and subverts them like having nancy be the sharpshooter is a way authors do this through media1 on a metalevel all of the popculture nostalgia references make the show successful in communication with hollywood decision makers who greenlit the show and audiences but cultural tropes are viewed as an incomplete representation of truth just like the story about the flea or the flipped game board is an incomplete representation of the nature of the upsidedown nancy isnt in band as steve assumes for example she also shoots well so cultural roles and tropes are useful but we have to be mindful of their incomplete nature and be willing to explore the strange but even stronger than compromise allowing something else to become familiar with oneself leaves you more open to manipulation by necessity the collective in the view of stranger things is defined by the mutual manipulation of its components sometimes voluntary and sometimes through deceitful manipulation the fact that deceit was used to positive effect in the case of murrays tape is strong proof that stranger things doesnt see this as inherently evil another case was lucass deceit in getting max isolated to tell her the secrets of the party or the party lying to the teacher about el or dustin manipulating dart there are many positive uses of deceit and manipulation in the show but this manipulation has to be done to advance the collective will of the group otherwise you are a virus technically a parasite but the show seems to equate virus with parasite something that uses the tools and machinery of a collective system for its own ends and not the collective good of the system going back to the discussion of reasons not to unite in familiar friendship dr brenner is the paragon of manipulation in the show he represents a virus figuratively in the way the mind flayer is literally a virus in wills body haha cute just caught that a virus inserts itself into the collective will 2 getting rid of him means exposing the divergence in his values from the groups values ie monsters dont like light or exposes written by murray right now im not sure what heat represents perhaps closeness and emotional warmth that seems to fit the heat lamps but the flamethrowers seem to be more anger and retribution as well as joyces face when she cranks the heat lamp focusing on the necessity of compromise and manipulation by others puts stranger things into conflict with the existential idea of radical choice while this is enough to establish conflict with existential ideas the show is very self aware of this and addresses the issue surprisingly directly we actually see in s2e7 the most direct confrontation of these ideas to date and we will definitely see the story of chasing down retired lab employees play out further when janeel lets the fat former lab employee go we see stranger things begin making an argument rejecting the existentialist conclusion that following orders is an invalid excuse the change in the relationship between the main characters and hawkins lab between seasons also illustrates an antiexistentialist willingness to forgive at least some employees who participated in pretty reprehensible things under the guise of following orders kali is a hindu god of anger revenge and freedom if el is supposed to represent the judeochristian deity he is described in sacred texts as compassionate and gracious slow to anger and abundant in mercy although jealous biblical texts rarely refer to the deity as angry wrathful or vengeful but his jealousy is often mentioned and we definitely see this in el regarding maxinemadmax but maybe the most interesting confrontation of existentialist individualistic radical choice is the relationship between jon and nancy where their bond of intimacy is deeper and more meaningful due to historical circumstance than the relationship she has with steve it is specifically framed as not a matter of her choice when they are getting drunk with murray she would consciously choose steve but she is forced by circumstance history is specifically cited by murray to fall in love with jon this is echoed in the scene with lucass little sister who forces lucass heman doll to fall in love with her doll her inevitable interference in lucass affairs snooping in his room as a product of circumstance living in the same house as siblings is another representation of the inability of lucas to choose to remain more distant stranger things argues against existentialism very strongly that identity and actions are a product of historical circumstance and that individuals are largely a product of their social environment i guess we should expect this from a pair of very close brothers 1 as a digression from the point i am making in the body of the post id like to discuss the use of metatextual reference in stranger things and westworld repetition according to stranger things happens in culture because the old is used to introduce the new because of familiarity in westworlds view the repetition in culture through history is due to the fact there are fundamental commonalities to human experience across time and culture westworld points to the example of orion the constellation as an example where many human cultures point to the same natural phenomenon and create culture around it in the view of stranger things there wouldnt necessarily be a cross cultural commonality of repeated themes the things repeated in the st view are repeated simply by virtue of happening before they are as random and arbitrary as what family you are born into or who your neighbors were growing up this is another thematic contrast between stranger things and a big popular show 2 another digression the contrast between el and kali seems to setup el as symbolic of the judeochristian deity will is setup as parallel character to el in s1 chiefly being absent when she is present and vice versa they seem to represent the two halves of forming successful union familiarity to the point of being able to manipulate others represented by el and her literal ability to manipulate objects and forming a collective will with common interests represented by the quiet kid who is always willing to go along with what others say and is able to hide himself symbolic of hiding his own internal desires and ego very fatherjesus like especially considering that will is also known as zombie boy this does seem to suggest a possible third element although the holy spirit could be the community of friends and family among the main characters

spoilers for the two abovementioned films and also vague spoilers for frankenstein and nineteen eightyfour parasite and us are two brilliant recent films that explore the relationship between the haves and have nots in society in both the underclass are represented as quite literally living underneath their wealthier counterparts either in a secret sewer tunnel network as with the tethered in us or the various belowground living spaces in parasite the poor familys semibasement apartment and the secret basement bunker of the wealthy park familys house in both films the storys trajectory is of this underclass rising up both literally and symbolically to replace the ruling classes whose prosperity made clear by the character red in her initial monologue comes as a direct consequence of others poverty both films explore the idea that being rich or poor is a matter of circumstance and the motif of the persecuted doppelganger coming to replace you in your comfortable life is an illustration of this both films also explore the ways that an underclass might be seen in society as subhuman in us the tethered are less than human in a literal sense being clones but the twist ending reveals that the character of ada who turned out to have been a tethered all along was able to integrate into society so well that as with blade runneresque scifi the line between human and nonhuman is rendered arbitrary in parasite multiple lines of dialogue compare the poor family to cockroaches and obviously the title of the film reflects the parasitical way those in poverty are forced to rely on the wealth and wifi of those who have money all of this of course has been inherited from mary shelleys frankenstein in which novel victor frankensteins creature is often seen as an allegorical manifestation of the masses rising up to take revenge on their superiors fuelled by anger and class resentment the french revolution had occurred only a few decades before frankenstein was written the idea of revolution fuels all of these stories in the very literal sense the word with the wheel of fortune revolving to empower the poor and disempower the rich the ending of the three stories might be a clue about their ideological message regarding class struggle in frankenstein victor frankensteins creature certainly rises up to take revenge but ultimately at no personal gain to himself and the revolution feels inevitable yet destructive and senseless in us the subhuman underclass rise up and succeed at replacing their ruling oppressors it is this story of the three i have mentioned that seems to be actively encouraging some kind of act of revolution parasite however is more like frankenstein the physical and emotional sufferings of the poorer characters are reiterated as the film goes on starvation humiliation displacement and the son character kiwoo is seen literally questioning whether he could truly belong in the kind of world his employers live in meanwhile kiwoos father kim cannot bear the idea that poverty seems to have infused his skin with a distinctive and permanent smell and the halfcrazed geunsae living a ratlike existence for 4 years under the house of the park family is unlike the tethered as his enslavement has led not to resentment towards his social superiors but reverence he seems to worship park as a form of deity geunsaes revenge is not against parks wealthy family but against the poor family whose actions led to the death of his wife but nonetheless his violence inspires kim to unleash his own bubbling class resentment directed towards park after the dust settles not only is the status quo resumed but the son kiwoo loses his sense of injustice entirely resolving to get a job get married and essentially join the system in order to one day have the money to buy the house and reunite with his father if it were not for the hugely sympathetic way the film represents the poor characters as victims of their environment it might be tempting to read this ending as being the exact opposite of us not a call for revolution but a reminder for citizens to know their place within capitalist class structures and to engage with the system rather than overturn it a sort of antimarxist sentiment however the grim final shot makes it clear that this idea of becoming rich and buying the house is a ridiculous dream and i see the ending more like the closing pages of orwells nineteen eightyfour the system has beaten and broken the protagonist who dared to take it on and has crushed them into conformity in this sense this may be an even bleaker ending than us since at least despite the destruction us puts forward the idea that things can change whereas in parasite things are static people live and die houses change hands but the rich and the poor stay where they are both physically and financially how do you see the endings of these films is the marxist reading of them the right approach here in your view ps i know i could have talked about other recent films relevant to this issue like high rise and boon jonghos previous film snowpiercer but the simple fact is i didnt like those films enough to bother analyse them i watched these two films back to back and i couldnt help but notice how two directors approach a similar theme differently i may be stretching it a bit much by comparing these two films that seem to have no real connection to each other but bare with me in this case id like to talk about the theme of class struggle inequality spoilers for snowpiercer joker and parasite it would seem appropriate to also include bong joonhos snowpiercer as it demonstrates a metaphorical microcosm of class warfare those of those poor class are huddled at the back of the train cornered like roaches with no where to go and its not until curtis breaks the illusion of power that they finally begin to fight back they manifest a revolution working their way up the train and breaking through the hooligans bong joonho born in daegu south korea in 1969 bong joonho is a rare example of a director who found success both abroad and in hollywood such a feat requires a rare mixture of vision dedication and appeal and bong joonho has those things in spades this year bong became the first korean director ever to win the palme dor at the cannes film festival but his career began much earlier back in south korea in 1990 bong completed a two year film studies program at the korea academy of film arts he showed early promise and two of his student films shorts titled memory within the frame and incoherence were showcased at the vancouver and hong kong international film festivals buoyed by this early acclaim bong shot his first feature as a director 2000s barking dogs never bite not garnering much attention right away the film grew on critics and it eventually played at the san sebastian film festival in spain and won awards at slamdance and the hong kong international film festival though it took years to break even the film showed bong had promise and his sophomore feature memories of murder an adaptation of a stage play about a real life serial killer was much larger in scope the festival circuit that afforded him early success for his shorts was eluding him for his features and cannes and venice passed on memoirs however san sebastian was keen to have him back and award him 3 prizes including best director bong spent the next few years working on shorts and one in particular influenza showcased bongs ability to make the ordinary seem unsettling with his use of real cctv cameras it was in 2006 when bong released the host that he began to get real tangible international acclaim the ambiguous title didnt dissuade audiences from coming out in droves to see this creature feature about a giant sea monster and the film racked up a few dozen international film awards keen to continue making unsettling films bong followed up the host with 2009s mother a taut psychological thriller about a mother determined to prove her sons innocence the entire world really took notice in 2013 when bong released his most ambitious film project to date snowpiercer set on a moving train in a frozen postapocalyptic earth snowpiercer fared poorly at us box offices on limited screens but doubled its budget back worldwide and made bong a hot commodity not wanting to squander this gravitas bong reunited with tilda swinton for 2017s okja which mitigated his last films tendency to underperform in us markets by being distributed by the digital platform netflix okja netted bong a nomination for best director at cannes that year and his followup 2019s parasite secured him the historic win his eye for the unsettling lack of reverence for the old guard in hollywood and pleasure in satirizing american cinema within the medium of american cinema have made him a favorite among indy film circles and his next project is eagerly awaited     excerpt of film hooligans bong sticks out to me moreso than anything else has not been reflected upon by anyone else either here nor in any other forum where i have read about the movie while watching parasite i was enthused by how the movie reminded me of a yugoslavian film from 1995 called underground it shares with it a house as a major character a basement wherein violence festers families that use and abuse each other for their mutual needs within the house a darkly comic tone and a tragic ending i came across underground in university when i was studying my undergraduate in english media culture studies i am sure there are far more educated minds here and wrote an essay on the film using psychoanalysis nationalism and memory as the prism through which i analysed it much of that analysis does not apply to parasite but i feel some of it does and has not been noticed by the much of the audience yet as the movie is still very fresh in the minds of the audience with that in mind i want to add a caveat i would like to reference along with the overall plot but before i get into them i would like you to try to understand the house as society mind or the national korean psyche furthermore i would like you to imagine that the basement is the area of that psyche wherein freuds id exists the animalistic uncontrollable lustful violent aspects of our psyche furthemore and more importantly for this text the id is the site of repressed trauma the id has been shown on screen as an underground area in many texts underground as ive mentioned true detective silence of the lambs i am sure you can think of countless examples of when a subterranean area has been the site wherein these types of actions take place or wherein a repressed trauma exists and boils to the surface think of any creature feature you like in parasite the id is dormant guense it is controlled and it has all its needs and demands taken care of this is done by the ego moongwang of the psyche that which moderates reality in order to appease these needs and demands the idealism of the text is derived from the house itself it is the house that inspires the occupants to develop moralistic and spiritualistic goals superego the park family too act as inspiration for the id and ego in the sense that they provide for both materially and spiritually mr park as deity to the other characters this fine balance is disrupted by the kim family once moongwang has been removed from her position the relationship between the id and superego has been broken and the needs of the id are no longer being met this gives rise to the id which invariably ascends from the subterranean with violence bringing with it its deep seated trauma resulting in the tragic violence we see at the birthday party only once the kim family has been removed from the surface level of the house can the id be forced back underground however this time in a different guise as mr kim the question then for scholars of culture what is the trauma that lurks underground i agree with the many people who take the socioeconomic outlook to this film i mean it is everywhere it is in the two houses it is in the dynamics of the relationships between the characters and it is in the motivations and prejudices of the characters but there are two scenes in this film that suggest there is more to the underground that what we see on the surface level of the text these scenes may contradict that analysis but i will suggest later that they compliment it in a way that perhaps is not immediately obvious scene 1 when guense and moongwang have the kim family hostage moongwang goes off on a monologue mimicking the north korean news readers this is a clear reference to the conflict between north and south korea however that alone could not justify an entirely new outlook on the film it needs something else to compliment or add further texture to it in order for it to be worth analyzing scene 2 later in the film mr kim finds guense in the basement headbutting a switch on the wall it turns out he is attempting to communicate with the surface level inhabitants through morse code a military language he says to mr kim a man of your age should understand it he expects mr park to understand it also and dasong also because he is in the scouts this scene does not reference north korea directly so i would like to abandon north korea specifically as the object of analyses but why are these two scenes there one an extensive and hilarious monologue the other a plot vehicle that doesnt have its intended purpose on the surface level what these two scenes have in common is a representation of a militaristic background why should men of a certain age or boys who are part of a certain institution be the only ones to be part of the communication why are the north korean news readers making an appearance in perhaps the most crucial part of the film and importantly when guense is on the surface level of the house it is my my hypothesis that the trauma in the id of this film the continued fear of potential war a fear that has been repressed over decades moderated appeased and controlled by the ego and transformed into something idealistic by the super ego which is represented by the beautiful house and the successful park family and it is the parasite that undoes this delicate balancing act by breaking the psychotic relationship between the three leading to violence the kim family are the parasites that threaten society as it exists they are the existential threat to peace in south korea by extension this article is illustrated with a lot of film stills if you would like to see the illustrated version click here i teach cinema in college i generally love and appreciate my students but please allow me a small rant each semester i have a few students who see every movie i show them as having a positive and uplifting message no matter how dark bloody or pessimistic the film may be they think it has a cliché disney moral one student wrote with the film apocalypse now the theme that stood out to me more than anything was to enjoy the little things what i mean by that is dont take things for granted and if doing something or having something makes you happy no matter how small it is cherish it i know what youre thinking and yes he was watching the right movie i checked sometimes they watch apocalypto by mistake i suppose that would be seen as a romcom about a guy finally getting the girl in the end heres another clint eastwoods unforgiven is about friendship and loyalty munny is dedicated to find justice for his partner ned im not sure i would call munnys murdering the sheriff and all his men justice but that was my students take away a different semester a student summarized unforgiven this way in the end the movie was about how a man overcomes guilt and learns a lesson of responsibility last semester i showed my class parasite and got three papers saying the film was about the importance of family eg kim kitaek is a caring and compassionate father who wants to fight for his family the movie shows how unity can make it easier for a family to confront their problems and bong joonho did a great job displaying the point that no matter where you come from if you work hard for what you want andor work together to make something happen it is possible to succeed here is an excerpt from a paper on pans labyrinth the major theme in the film of pans labyrinth is the theme of motherhoodthe other theme is the theme of kindness which in the film is portrayed by maribel verdu who is loving kindhearted caring ok but what about the fascist captain smashing a mans face in with a wine bottle in a paper comparing the piano and like water for chocolate a student wrote that the movies shared a common message no matter what the person goes through theres always a way to find a solution to it it was a happy ending for both of them a student wrote in a paper about us jordan peele wanted to show that when a family comes together and work to make things happen they have the ability to come out successful no matter who you are the vast majority of my students do not maniacally smack a smiley face on everything but i cant get through a semester without at least a few happygolucky loons sprinkling glittery happy dust everywhere it reminds me of the happy happy joy joy episode of ren and stimpy america has a reputation for being a country of optimists it is seen as the land of big smiles and big bellies i dont know if this characterization is accurate but the reputation must have come from somewhere most likely americas sunny outlook stems from americas promise of a classless society where anyone can better their status our culture is built on the accumulated hopes of millions of immigrants whether america fulfills its promise is a different story altogether but the dream persists the towering skyscrapers still shine even if at their base they reek of urine and are surrounded by homeless people kim kardashian still wiggles with glossy sex even if underneath her skin is a frankensteins horror of silicone scars and collagen ronald mcdonald still smiles even if he is peddling us meatflavored toxic sludge its a strange country that we live in but some of those immigrants do find a better life some people do pull themselves up by their bootstrap at least enough of them to keep the dream alive optimism can be a healthy driver of change but only to the extent that it does not obscure reality my students are young and they have made it into college they see the world before them and are optimistic about their future many of them have experienced challenging circumstances growing up and i have found that in order to face lifes challenges they have been raised on a diet of positive cliches if you can dream it you can do it dont let what other people say bother you god never gives you more than you can handle everything happens for reason life is 10 what happens to you and 90 how you react to it these sayings help bolster their efforts and foster optimism but unfortunately there is a point of diminishing returns its not just that these saying may cause people to misgauge their capabilities but they may misjudge the capabilities of others as well i often find that the same optimism that enables an individual to move forward is accompanied by a lack of empathy for those who fail to thrive tell me i can do anything i set my mind to and it may motivate me but if i truly accept this statement i may well conclude that the people who find themselves on the margins of society have only themselves to blame i remember arguing with a black student who insisted that the slaves had a choice and should have done something instead of submitting shen was defending this position because she was protecting her own hopes of a better life for herself if peoples efforts can be hampered by difficult circumstances then her outlook would be bleak  films like unforgiven and us provide an opportunity to reexamine americas mythology the purpose of movies like these is to question ideas like bootstraps and the twocar garage however if the audience has been raised on marvel disney and apple pie they have a powerful and transformative lens that can turn tragedy into triumph i see the posts on this sub and get really impressed by how good many of you are at analyzing movies and putting forth exciting theories and reflections i consider myself to be above the average film goer but not much more i find that writing down thoughts on the movie as well as rewatching it really helps me get somewhere but can i become so good that a one time watch is enough to have a strong analysis of the moviemostly interested in theme and story not so much cinematography etc i find that i struggle with being to literal example i thought uncut gems was about greed and never really thought of it as a movie about chaos i also didnt really understand parasite so its a family employing themselves to work for another family what am i supposed to draw from that that they are like a parasite yeah sure does that make it the best movie of the year not for me these are just examples to try to illustrate where im struggling when interpreting a movie but it really bothers me that i dont understand these widely successful movies so do you have any tips would love some input what are some of your favorite films that feature architecture almost as a character rather than a backdrop a few that come to mind are gattaca parasite and blade runner some brief thoughts gattaca architecture is used as an element that reflects the pursuit of perfection minimalist and brutalist buildings by frank lloyd wright and antoine predock are featured and while they appear futuristic the detail that goes into these buildings rather than set pieces lends to the grounded familiarity that makes gattaca seem believable this is particularly evident in wrights marin county civic center where every single detail from the doors windows and even the furniture was designed by wright as well the building was designed with the idea that it should last 300 years so it was imagined as something that would fit in within a futuristic context parasite parasite is interesting because it relies on fictional architecture as a production designer lee ha jun had to approach the design of the park house from an architects point of view as to create a home that audiences could believe was designed by a famous architect the first level of the house was actually built while the second level was added digitally one of the key features is how the house responds to the sun this enhances the idea that people at the top in society have more access to sunlight while people at the bottom live mostly in shadow along with that idea the most recognizable use of architecture is how the contrast between the park home and the kims semibasement apartment highlights the staggering difference between the rich and the poor blade runner this one features architecture more as an environment so aside from featuring frank lloyd wrights ennis house and key scenes in the bradbury building the film features architecture at a much larger scale syd mead designed the world by imagining a future that grows upward the street level would then become a basement that people would not want to inhabit according to mead life essentially starts at a lobby on the 30th floor and anything below that is significantly less desirable furthermore there is clear influence from large ceremonial structures seen throughout history you could argue that this is still architecture as a backdrop but it would be hard to imagine blade runner without also imagining such an incredible environment im sure there are plenty of details i missed in these three any other thoughts what other films depict architecture in a fascinating way this article is illustrated with a lot of film stills if you would like to see the illustrated version click here the thing was finally released in 1982 after eight messy years of machinations it was directed by john carpenter and written and rewritten by bill lancaster when it came out it flopped with audiences and critics alike i was 14 then and it was a big hit with me especially the scene where they test each others blood in petrie dishes the thing is essentially about distrust and paranoia it is set in the arctic where the characters are already in peril just by being there they must rely on each other to survive and when the fabric of their trust is stressed by fear and suspicion it begins to unravel the thing had been put on hold for over a year when the success of ridley scotts alien in 1979 helped revitalize the project alien and the thing share common themes having to do with claustrophobic terror and the invasion of our bodies by an intrusive interloper the thing adds paranoia to the mix by giving its monster the ability to disguise itself as anyone or anything the blood test scene in the thing comes at the apex of this paranoia the group is degenerating and soon it will be every man for himself they are trapped with each other and all trust has been erased there is no longer anything holding them back from killing one another in fact they have already begun the blood test is the only hope left of preserving some sense of cohesion they gather in the science lab to perform the test that they hope will reveal who is human and who is not four men are tied up on a couch and two are standing free holding flame throwers the plan is to have everyone give a sample of blood in a petrie dish and then test it kurt russells character macready is the de facto leader his leadership position was not established through consensus but by brute force and luck it falls to him to place a hot wire in each petrie dish if the blood reacts defensively they will know it belongs to the alien  no one knows what the blood will do if it is alien they assume it will try to shrink away from the heat no one even knows if the test will work no one knows what to do once the experiment is done there was no time to form a consensus or a carefully considered plan the experiment is only designed to be a next step forward in a direction they have not fully considered it is always of critical importance in a horror movie whether the audience has less or more or the same amount of knowledge that the protagonist and other characters have in this scene we are all equally unprepared in addition it is not clear whether a person who has been replaced by the thing would know that they are an imposter or not is everyone in the room equally in the dark or is someone ahead of the game the scene is akin to the climactic drawingroom scenes in all those agatha christie novels everyone is gathered for the big reveal as the process of elimination proceeds we continually rewrite our explanations to fit the unfolding facts perhaps it is partly the familiarity with such scenes that draws us off our guard as we watch macready stick a heated wire in each dish of blood as much as there is the mounting tension there is also the feeling of impending resolution  each character shows relief as he is eliminated from the list of suspects they watch each other and are sometimes surprised or disappointed to see that someone they suspected is actually innocent this is an opportunity to more closely examine what suspicion and paranoia are and how they effect us both individually and as a group each of them must allow themselves to be cut by a scalpel and have their blood drawn this sacrifice is made begrudgingly as most of them possibly all of them are sure they are innocent the scene progresses and with each innocent result the tension is allowed to at least partially defuse the first scare is simply a jump scare when the blood leaps out of the dish and splashes on the floor the next scare begins a cascade of unexpected consequences the blood begins to slip and slide toward the men the men on the couch struggle to keep away from the animated blood immediately the scene goes from bad to worse when you remember that the men are tied up they had been tied up to prevent them from running away or attacking each other no one had anticipated this scenario including the audience  the realization that they cant get away is just tickling our adrenal glands when all hell breaks loose palmer the man sitting all the way on one end of the couch begins to twitch and convulse and everyone starts to scream now its special effects artist rob bottins time to shine bottin was completely dedicated to the film he worked himself past the point of exhaustion and ended up with double pneumonia   palmer begins to transform his eyes bulge and his head swells and soon he is covered in blood macreadys flame thrower stalls the choice to arm macready and another character named windows with flame throwers not only provides an unreliable weapon but an extremely inexact one the flame throwers are almost as unpredictable and terrifying as the monster the camera quickly cuts between closeups of the failed flamethrower nozzle palmers gory head and medium shots of the men screaming in panic as they try to escape their bonds now that the tension has erupted into fear carpenter keeps upping the ante the beast attacks windows biting and crushing him the horror of what is happening plays out in front of a double audience we share the terror of the tiedup men as they struggle and watch the creature flail around trying to swallow windows macready struggles with the flamethrower while blood spatters everywhere the quickly paced editing leaves the audience no time to breathe the chaos lasts for 60 terrifying seconds and then carpenter cuts to a wide shot as macready shoots his flame thrower the fire lights up the room and we have a second to take it all in its all an unanticipated chain of disasters first the blood unexpectedly jumps out of the dish second it crawls across the floor third the men cant escape fourth the flamethrower is unreliable and now fifth by setting the monster on fire it has become even more dangerous as it stumbles toward macready fortunately the monster veers off course and smashes through the wall it lumbers out into the snow and dies in a flaming heap anthony cecere is the stuntman who risked his life in more ways than one to make these incredible 15 seconds of film palmer dying in the snow is the apex of the scene afterward there are a few more tests and the dispatching of what is left of windows but by then the tension has subsided the craftsmanship and timing keep this scene tense for an exceptionally long time prolonging tension and fear is very difficult an audience can get overwhelmed and just disengage or you can give the audience too much time to think things through creating distance between them and the characters you can not count on the initial scare to do all the work the scene has to twist or intensify in some way  the thing was scored by the incomparable ennio morricone but there is no music at all during the entire blood test scene music might have brought some kind of overarching order or predictability to a scene that relies on continually ratcheting up the chaos movies like the remake of invasion of the body snatchers which came out 4 years before the thing and alien which came out 3 years before made the wellestablished alien invasion trope personal instead of flying saucers facing a line of tanks invasion of the body snatchers the thing and alien focus on the individual body as the site of conflict almost all horror films exploit our fear of the other if you want to scare a human you can count on a primal tribal fear of the outsider whether it is the witchburning in salem the inquisition in spain the holocaust in germany the huac trials or the maga horde its the same expression of the same emotion but its all made worse when the focus is not on the tribe but on an individual body the threat is far more immediate and palpable when your own body is at stake michael myers jason and chucky are external antagonists that can be located and labeled as outsiders but the thing not only threatens our safety it threatens a fate worse than death a loss of identity this is the trumpists ultimate fear they are scared that the mexicans or the muslims will change what it means to be an american these foreigners will change our language the food we eat and the legal system they will take our jobs our neighborhoods and soon the american norm will no longer be the white middleclass christian it will be something foreign in 2018 alex garland made a film called annihilation garlands film has a lot in common with carpenters in fact garland appears to make a direct reference to the thing in a scene where a group of women is accosted by an alien beast just as in the thing the women have all been tied to chairs by one of their own party just as in the thing the women no longer trust each other and are afraid that one of them has been infected just as in the thing there are unexpected consequences that terrify everyone involved including the audience in annihilation the cast is entirely female in the thing it is entirely male the scenes are similar but the challenge for the women is to be able to keep calm and silent in order to prevent provoking the beast for the men its all out in the open where there is chaos and panic for the men there is teeth grinding restraint for the women this difference could be seen as reflecting cultural ideas about how different genders are socialized to deal with stress but either way both scenes result in an explosion of panicked violence in all four films invasion of the body snatchers the thing alien and annihilation biology and nature are used instead of the supernatural all of them focus on the immediacy of our bodies without concerning themselves with anything spiritual or otherworldly these are visceral movies that confront us directly the title the were foreigners having their own take on tokyo the common theme i found between those three films was social isolation which is particularly fitting because of the worldwide lockdown we are experiencing right now in interior design we follow a young couple crashing at a friends place while they attempt to manage the financial struggles of adult life the girl hiroko supports her boyfriends akira dream of becoming a filmmaker and is unsuccessfully searching for a place to live in while akira lands a small job and screens his first directorial debut hiroko feels left out and useless and slowly disappearstransforms into a literal chair this first kafkaesque story explores the theme of individualism and adds a feminist touch by sharing the alienation of a woman unable to establish an identity for herself her boyfriend is an artist who is not faced by the same worries as hers undermined by her friend and even her own boyfriend she is the pragmatic girlfriend in charge of solving the couples financial problems i liked the subtle commentary on sex work throughout the movie a taboo that also reflects underlying problems such as unemployment and the commodification of women or even just anyone really in todays society the fact that hiroko ends up accepting and embracing her existence as a chair in the end is particularly striking since it is the first time she can actually feel like a useful part of society she is ultimately a spectator who unlike her boyfriend a creative type wanting to be under the spotlight is content to be a fly on the wall or rather a bird in a cage leos caraxs merde tells the story of a leprechaunlike sewerliving creature terrorizing the people of tokyo alagodzilla creating a climate of fear and terror socalled merde is captured by the authorities and defended by a french lawyer the only person capable to communicate with him at trials after sparking a national debate in japan he is sentenced to death until a divine intervention changes the course of events caraxs film is a collage of different themes from terrorism to nationalism japans shunned war atrocities cultural shock the media mass hysteria and conformity it is hard to pinpoint a specific message since merde himself is quite an ambiguous creature we dont know whether his hatred is specific to japan or people in general although carax might have wanted to portray the consequences of war and historical trauma as merde states that the japanese raped his mother quite literally like a bomb merde shakes up japan and brings its citizens to reflect on the countrys bloody history instead of hiding behind its polite facade on the other hand he also revives a nationalist movement by becoming a common target this film shows how history is shaped by a vicious circle of generational and intercultural violence how violence can be crafted in the very language we speak as well as understood by people all around the world and how empathy as demonstrated by the french lawyers interactions with merde might help us set aside and ultimately blind us from our differences the last film shaking tokyo follows a hikikomori who lives on his dads money and never leaves his attribute to kiwoo he was the only one that saw any meaning behind the thing the latter was attributed to kitaek surely if the logical explanation for the poverty stink was because of the familys living conditions kiwoo should have it as well however there seems to be a careful construction in the script to make sure that these symbols are not interchangeable as i rewatched it a second time i tool known as chekhovs gun which is a dramatic principle that states every element in a story must be necessary basically if your movie introduces an element it must be referenced back to otherwise designed by bongs directing skill besides the two most obvious metaphors the rock and the stink bong deliberately scatters more subtle ones across the movie he uses blocking techniques to frame the cube was fun the cube was a little puzzle to play with the platform is not a game not unless you like very bloody bloody games the platform is a metaphor in the tradition of animal farm or lord of the flies the platform very clearly focuses on capitalist ideology and has a secondary biblical theme as well to be fair the cube did what it set out to do it was a science fiction thriller with an interesting premise some tense scenes and some amount of political commentary the platform sets its goals higher the premise of the platform is that people are sentenced to prison terms in an immensely tall tower with cement rooms piled one on top of the other down the center of the building is a shaft every day a large platform makes its way down the shaft on the platform is a sumptuous feast of the finest delicacies prepared with the highest standards the platform descends layer by layer through the rooms and each prisoner can take as much as they want if the first 50 rooms are greedy they leave nothing for the many more below them the platform depicts a highly artificial environment full of shocking absurdity that we quickly come to recognize as not that distant from our own environment the film distills and exaggerates in order to resonate with and then amplify currents and forces in our social and political reality it is essential that it be both foreign and grotesque but at the same time familiar we are presented with a cross section of society that may or may not deserve to be in prison the longer an individual has been in the prison the more deranged and depraved they become regardless of their backgrounds they are equalized by desperation the platform was made in spain and directed by galder gazteluurrutia in 2019 the challenge with the film is to create something rich out of very limited elements the platform has only one location and a limited number of actors it is limited by its own design on one hand the paired down presentation makes its points more forcefully the more bloody and horrific the film gets the more it exposes the violence hidden away from middle and upperclass society on the other hand the stark nature of the film limits its ability to deal with nuance there were definitely aspects that recalled bunuel films the isolation of the exterminating angel in which the rich party guests cannot leave after indulging in a decadent feast similarly the discreet charm of the bourgeoisie centers on a feast that the guests never manage to eat in viridiana the feast takes on religious symbolism but degenerates into absurdity they all are centered on consumption as well as the segregation of the classes they all depict the rich living in a rarefied and exquisite world that is for all its luxury isolated by its own blind selfindulgence however unlike bunuels films the platform is not surreal it is an allegory i have seen the platform described as a dystopian film but it is not it is an illustration of contemporary society it does not warn us of where we are headed it shows us where we are it is not a world building movie it is a parable that requires a bizarre conceit in order to elucidate what is already in front of our eyes in addition unlike many dystopian films the platform wrestles good and hard with a variety of solutions to the problems it presents karl marx adam smith milton friedman and noam chomsky battle it out through grimy proxies struggling in a torrent of cake blood roast beef and shattered glass the device of setting a film in a literal hierarchy seems to be an increasingly common theme in contemporary film there is the lighthouse us parasite and snowpiercer which is sort of a hierarchy laid on its side these films are all designed to bring to the surface a world that is deliberately kept secret and apart from the one we live in of all of them the platform is the most graphic and gruesome in many ways it is the most overtly political of the group it is its forceful nature that pushes aside some of the nuance i mentioned earlier but at the same time the film makes a clear and powerful statement about class economics and struggle in addition hierarchical architecture invokes religious imagery there is the physical hierarchy of heaven and hell with us trapped in between there is the ranked hierarchy with god at the top then the angels beneath him then humans and then animals since the setting is spain we also have the intricate and many layered hierarchy of the catholic bishops and cardinals and priests there is also a bitter tie between the church and the kind of conservative cutthroat capitalism depicted in the platform the catholic church sided with franco during the spanish civil war as it did with hitler in germany in agurrie the wrath of god carvajal the franciscan monk explains to a noblewoman you know my child for the good of our lord the church was always on the side of the strong the wound inflicted by this reprehensible union is something that is still felt in spain germany and in italy as well christianity capitalism and exploitation have been the closest of bedfellows for almost two centuries agurrie after all was a conquistador his venal motivations were masked by the monks presence as a representative of the church carvajal eschews the material world of riches and seeks to save the heathen savages at least that is what he was told to say the people that populate the platform have devolved into savages there is little room for god or any system of of values that transcends the material
